title: user message
summary: **Constraints:** - Use only two sub-structures. **Goal:** - Build a single rocket engine capable of providing propulsion to a single direction. - Build a simple chassis to assemble the rocket engines using braces to for…
body:
**Constraints:**
- Use only two sub-structures.

**Goal:**
- Build a single rocket engine capable of providing propulsion to a single direction.
- Build a simple chassis to assemble the rocket engines using braces to form a symmetric rocket.
- The assembled rocket should be able to lift off from the ground to the sky in the simulation environment.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The assembled rocket will be placed at position (x=0, y=0, z=0) on the ground plane.
- During the simulation, the firing control key of the rocket engine will be pressed and held continuously.
- The motion trajectory of the assembled rocket will be recorded throughout the simulation.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Maximum Height:* The highest vertical position (z) reached by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Trajectory Deviation:* The average lateral distance between the assembled rocket’s actual trajectory and the ideal vertical line (smaller is better).
- *Maximum Speed:* The highest speed achieved by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Cost:* The total number of blocks used to construct the assembled rocket (fewer is better).

title: planner message
summary: <building_plan> <overall_structure> <description> The final rocket consists of two identical steam-powered rocket engine sub-structures mounted symmetrically to a central chassis sub-structure using braces. Each engine…
body:
<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      The final rocket consists of two identical steam-powered rocket engine sub-structures mounted symmetrically to a central chassis sub-structure using braces. Each engine is a compact vertical stack: a stone base, two small wooden blocks stacked upward, a downward-pointing water cannon mounted under the upper wooden block, and a torch mounted on the top face of the upper wooden block pointing downward to heat the water cannon inlet for steam mode. The central chassis is a simple vertical column (stone base plus two small wooden blocks stacked upward) that provides two clear side faces at the same heights as the engine’s two wooden blocks. Two braces per engine connect the engine’s wooden blocks to the corresponding wooden blocks on the chassis, creating a rigid, symmetric rocket with both engines producing upward thrust when fired.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      When both water cannons fire, their spray is directed downward; their recoil produces upward thrust. The torches heat the cannons’ inlet ends so each cannon operates in steam mode, multiplying thrust by 8.6 and providing ample lift. Symmetric placement around the chassis ensures the net thrust vector passes close to the overall center of mass, minimizing lateral forces and trajectory deviation. The braces hold the engines rigidly in mirrored positions so the rocket lifts vertically with minimal yaw or roll.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      Build the two sub-structures independently. Replicate the engine unit twice. Place the central chassis at world center. Position the two engines equidistant to the east and west of the chassis. Connect the engines to the chassis using four braces: upper-left and lower-left braces for the west engine, and upper-right and lower-right braces for the east engine. This forms a single symmetric rocket assembly.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      Configure both water cannons to the same firing control (e.g., Fire). During evaluation, hold the firing control continuously. No wheels or other motion blocks are used. Expect a strong upward acceleration from rest and a predominantly vertical trajectory due to symmetry and rigid bracing.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Steam Rocket Engine Unit</name>
      <description>
        A single-direction rocket engine that produces upward thrust via a downward-pointing water cannon operated in steam mode by a downward-facing torch heating its inlet end. It is built as a vertical stack so the nozzle sits above ground, avoiding collisions at liftoff and ensuring the torch is safely out of the steam plume.
      </description>
      <components>
        - Base: Default 1x1x1 stone block (mass 0.25)
        - Structural: Small Wooden Block A (1x1x1, mass 0.3), Small Wooden Block B (1x1x1, mass 0.3)
        - Propulsion: Water Cannon (1.75x1x1, mass 1.5)
        - Heat Source: Torch (1.5x0.5x0.5, mass 1.0)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        Build relative to the engine’s own base stone (centered initially at its own origin; later positioned in the overall assembly):
        - Step 1: Attach Small Wooden Block A to the top (z+) face of the base stone. This places Block A’s center 0.5 units above the base’s top face center (A’s center ends at z = base center z + 1.0).
        - Step 2: Attach Small Wooden Block B to the top (z+) face of Block A so Block B’s center is at z = base center z + 2.0.
        - Step 3: Attach the Water Cannon to the bottom (z−) face of Block B, oriented to point straight down (negative z). The cannon’s center sits 0.5 units below Block B’s bottom face; its outlet end is 1.0 unit below the cannon center and its inlet end is 0.75 unit above the cannon center.
          • With base center at z = 0.5 (ground), Block A center at z = 1.5 and Block B center at z = 2.5, the cannon center is at z = 1.5; the outlet end is at z = 0.5 (safely above ground), and the inlet end is at z = 2.25.
        - Step 4: Attach the Torch to the top (z+) face of Block B, oriented to point straight down (negative z). The torch body sits 0.5 units above Block B’s top face, and the flame center is 1.0 unit below the torch body center, placing the heating sphere center at z = 2.5. This is 0.25 units above the water cannon inlet (z = 2.25), so the 0.3-radius heating sphere overlaps the inlet end. This reliably triggers steam mode while keeping the torch clear of the downward steam plume.
        - Reserve faces for bracing: Leave Block A’s west/east faces and Block B’s west/east faces free for brace connections to the chassis in the final assembly.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - Control: Configure this engine’s Water Cannon to respond to the shared Fire control input. No control is needed for the torch; it remains lit until extinguished (placement above the nozzle minimizes extinguishing risk).
        - Expected behavior: When fired, the cannon produces steam-powered recoil upward (~13.76 units of mass-equivalent thrust per cannon). The engine provides thrust in a single direction (up) continuously.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides upward propulsion. One unit is used for the east side and one identical unit for the west side of the rocket to achieve symmetric thrust.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - Keep the water cannon axis exactly vertical (pointing down) for maximum vertical lift.
        - Ensure the torch’s heating sphere overlaps the water cannon inlet end to trigger steam mode while staying out of the downward plume.
        - Maintain Block A and Block B side faces unoccupied for brace connection to the chassis.
        - The lowest part of the cannon (outlet) must remain at or above z = 0.5 so nothing intersects the ground at placement.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>

    <sub_structure_2>
      <name>Symmetric Central Chassis</name>
      <description>
        A minimal vertical frame to which two identical engine units are braced symmetrically to the east and west. The chassis aligns its side faces at the same two heights as the engine’s stacked wooden blocks for rigid dual-point bracing per engine. This maintains symmetry and reduces trajectory deviation.
      </description>
      <components>
        - Base: Default 1x1x1 stone block (mass 0.25)
        - Structural: Small Wooden Block C (1x1x1, mass 0.3), Small Wooden Block D (1x1x1, mass 0.3), stacked vertically
        - Connectors: 4 Braces (mass 0.5 each) for engine-to-chassis links (two per engine, upper and lower)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        Build relative to the chassis base stone and then assemble engines:
        - Step 1: Place the chassis base stone so its bottom rests at z = 0 (world origin for the assembled rocket). Its center is at (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0.5).
        - Step 2: Attach Small Wooden Block C to the chassis base’s top (z+) face; C’s center at z = 1.5.
        - Step 3: Attach Small Wooden Block D to the top (z+) face of C; D’s center at z = 2.5.
        - Step 4: Position the two completed Engine Units:
          • East Engine: place its base stone so its center is at (x = +3.0, y = 0.0, z = 0.5). This sets its Block A center at z = 1.5 and Block B center at z = 2.5; the water cannon outlet at z = 0.5.
          • West Engine: place its base stone so its center is at (x = −3.0, y = 0.0, z = 0.5), mirroring the east engine.
        - Step 5: Connect Braces (each between free faces of wooden blocks):
          • East lower brace: from the West face center of East Engine Block A (x = +2.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5) to the East face center of Chassis Block C (x = +0.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5).
          • East upper brace: from the West face center of East Engine Block B (x = +2.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5) to the East face center of Chassis Block D (x = +0.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5).
          • West lower brace: from the East face center of West Engine Block A (x = −2.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5) to the West face center of Chassis Block C (x = −0.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5).
          • West upper brace: from the East face center of West Engine Block B (x = −2.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5) to the West face center of Chassis Block D (x = −0.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5).
        This creates two rigid, mirrored brace pairs, holding both engines symmetrically on either side of the central chassis.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - No active motion blocks in the chassis. It provides passive structural alignment. Ensure both engines’ water cannons are mapped to the same Fire control input to operate simultaneously.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides structural integrity and a symmetry reference for mounting the engines. The dual-height bracing per engine reduces flex, keeps thrust vectors parallel and centered, and minimizes trajectory deviation.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - Keep Block C and Block D side faces free and aligned with the engine Blocks A and B to accept braces.
        - Braces must connect between unoccupied wooden block faces, not to water cannons or torches.
        - Position the two engines equidistant (±3.0 in x) from the chassis centerline for symmetry.
        - Ensure all components’ lowest points remain at z ≥ 0 to avoid ground intersection.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_2>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>

title: drafter message
summary: 1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25) with center at z = 0.5 (ground reference) - starting reference 2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser above base; reserve west/east faces for later…
body:
1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25) with center at z = 0.5 (ground reference) - starting reference

2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser above base; reserve west/east faces for later bracing - top face of Block 1

3 - Small Wooden Block B - second vertical riser above A; reserve west/east faces for later bracing - top face of Block 2

4 - Water Cannon - downward-pointing nozzle attached under Block B; center sits 0.5 units below Block B’s bottom face; inlet end is 0.75 units above cannon center; outlet end is 1.0 unit below cannon center - bottom face of Block 3 - fires straight down (negative z); recoil thrust straight up; configure to respond to shared Fire control; operate in steam mode by heating inlet end

5 - Torch - downward-facing heater attached above Block B; torch body center sits 0.5 units above Block B’s top face; flame/heating sphere center sits 1.0 unit below torch body center (radius 0.3), overlapping the cannon inlet end - top face of Block 3 - flame points straight down (negative z); remains lit (no control); purpose is to heat the Water Cannon inlet to trigger steam mode while staying out of the downward plume

Assembly notes:
- With the base center at z = 0.5, Block A center at z = 1.5, and Block B center at z = 2.5:
  • Water Cannon center is at z = 1.5; outlet end at z = 0.5 (at/above ground requirement satisfied); inlet end at z = 2.25.
  • Torch body center is at z = 3.5; flame/heating sphere center at z = 2.5, which is 0.25 units above the inlet end (overlap within the 0.3 radius ensures steam mode).
- Keep west/east faces of Blocks A and B unoccupied to allow brace connections to the chassis during final assembly.
- Motion/control behavior: Water Cannon uses the shared Fire control input; Torch has no control and remains lit until extinguished.

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Review assumptions and coordinate frame - I take the base block’s center as (x=0, y=0, z=0.5). All listed components are stacked coaxially (same x, y as the base). Where face offsets are specified by the part definition…
body:
Review assumptions and coordinate frame
- I take the base block’s center as (x=0, y=0, z=0.5). All listed components are stacked coaxially (same x, y as the base). Where face offsets are specified by the part definitions (e.g., water cannon center is 0.5 away from the attached face along the face normal; torch body center is 0.5 away from its attached face along the face normal), I use those exactly.

Step-by-step structural check (placement, center coordinates, neighbor distances, overlap tests)

1) Base starting block (1x1x1)
- Center: (0, 0, 0.5); extents in z: [0.0, 1.0].
- Neighbors at this stage: none.
- Overlaps: none.

2) Small Wooden Block A (1x1x1)
- Attachment: on the base top face (z+).
- Center: z = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5; full center (0, 0, 1.5); extents in z: [1.0, 2.0].
- Distances to neighboring centers:
  • To Base: |Δz| = |1.5 − 0.5| = 1.0.
- Minimum required separation along z to avoid overlap: 0.5 (base half-height) + 0.5 (Block A half-height) = 1.0.
- Result: face-to-face contact only; no overlap. OK.
- Side-face reservation: West/East faces remain unoccupied. OK.

3) Small Wooden Block B (1x1x1)
- Attachment: on Block A top face (z+).
- Center: z = 1.5 + 1.0 = 2.5; full center (0, 0, 2.5); extents in z: [2.0, 3.0].
- Distances to neighboring centers:
  • To Block A: |Δz| = |2.5 − 1.5| = 1.0.
  • To Base: |Δz| = |2.5 − 0.5| = 2.0.
- Minimum required separation along z:
  • A–B: 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 → OK (face-contact, no overlap).
  • Base–B: 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 → 2.0 ≥ 1.0 → OK.
- Side-face reservation: West/East faces remain unoccupied. OK.

4) Water Cannon (length along firing axis = 1.75; width = 1; height = 1)
- Attachment: to Block B bottom face (z−), oriented to point straight down (−z).
- By spec: the cannon center is 0.5 units away from the attached face along the face normal. For a bottom face, that means 0.5 below the face.
- Block B bottom face is at z = 2.5 − 0.5 = 2.0 → Water Cannon center z = 2.0 − 0.5 = 1.5; full center (0, 0, 1.5).
- Axis endpoints (along z):
  • Inlet end: center + 0.75 → z = 1.5 + 0.75 = 2.25 (toward +z).
  • Outlet end: center − 1.0 → z = 1.5 − 1.0 = 0.5 (toward −z).
- Occupied z-span by cannon body: [0.5, 2.25].
- Distances to neighboring centers and overlap checks (use half-lengths along each axis):
  • Against Base: center separation |Δz| = |1.5 − 0.5| = 1.0.
    - Required along z: cannon half-length 0.875 + base half-height 0.5 = 1.375 → 1.0 < 1.375 → overlap in z.
    - In x/y, centers coincide and half-widths are 0.5 each → overlap in x and y as well.
    - Conclusion: Water Cannon overlaps the Base block (z overlap range is [0.5, 1.0]).
  • Against Block A: center separation |Δz| = |1.5 − 1.5| = 0.
    - Required along z: 0.875 + 0.5 = 1.375 → 0 < 1.375 → severe overlap in z.
    - In x/y, centers coincide → overlap in x and y.
    - Conclusion: Water Cannon volume overlaps Block A extensively (Block A z-range [1.0, 2.0] lies inside cannon z-span).
  • Against Block B: center separation |Δz| = |1.5 − 2.5| = 1.0.
    - Required along z: 0.875 + 0.5 = 1.375 → 1.0 < 1.375 → overlap in z.
    - In x/y, centers coincide → overlap in x and y.
    - Conclusion: Water Cannon intrudes 0.25 units into Block B above its bottom face (2.0 to 2.25).
- Attachment validity: While “attached to bottom face of Block B” is an allowed attachment, the cannon’s geometry always extends 0.25 above that face (because 0.75 above center − 0.5 offset = 0.25), guaranteeing intrusion into the host block. This violates the no-overlap requirement.

5) Torch (shape reported as [1.5, 0.5, 0.5]; functional offsets: 0.5 away from face along face normal, flame/heating sphere center 1.0 from body center along the torch’s shaft direction)
- Attachment: to Block B top face (z+), pointing straight down (−z).
- Torch body center: by spec, 0.5 above the top face → z = 3.0 + 0.5 = 3.5; full center (0, 0, 3.5).
- Heating sphere center: 1.0 below torch body center along −z → z = 3.5 − 1.0 = 2.5; radius = 0.3.
- Proximity to Water Cannon inlet:
  • Water Cannon inlet z = 2.25; Torch heating sphere center z = 2.5 → |Δz| = 0.25, x/y coincide.
  • 0.25 < 0.3 radius → inlet end lies inside the heating sphere. Heating coverage is achieved.
- Collision check (Torch vs Block B):
  • The torch support is offset 0.5 above the top face (outside the block); the vertical shaft extends downward in free space in front of the torch, not through the block. No geometric intrusion into Block B’s solid volume is implied by the given torch geometry. Acceptable (no overlap).
- Collision check (Torch vs Water Cannon):
  • Torch body center at z=3.5; cannon body spans [0.5, 2.25]. Separation is at least 1.25 in z; no overlap.

Structural integrity conclusion
- The Water Cannon placement creates multiple overlaps:
  • With Block B (intrudes 0.25 above the bottom face).
  • With Block A (overlaps its entire 1.0 ≤ z ≤ 2.0 span).
  • With the Base block (overlaps 0.5 ≤ z ≤ 1.0).
- Per the review requirements, any overlap must be flagged and causes failure. Therefore, the current sub-structure does not pass structural integrity.

Functional validation
- Orientation: The cannon is described to point straight down, which is correct for upward recoil. Torch is placed above and aimed down, and its heating sphere does overlap the inlet by 0.25 (< 0.3 radius), meeting the steam-mode heating requirement.
- Motion/control: Configured to shared Fire input; torch remains lit. In principle, this would work to generate steam-mode thrust (~13.76 mass-equivalent) if the structure were collision-free.
- Mounting and alignment: Despite correct orientation, the cannon’s mounting geometry into the bottom face inherently intrudes into the host block and, with the current stack, into neighboring blocks and the base. As mounted, motion would be obstructed by overlapping solids and is not verifiably feasible.

Physical feasibility
- Thrust and direction are theoretically correct. However, the cannon cannot physically occupy the specified space without colliding with the base and the risers. Steam operation might also heat or damage overlapping wooden blocks. Thus, the physical system is not feasible as currently assembled.

Specific reasons for failure and what must change
- Fundamental geometric conflict: A Water Cannon attached to a 1x1x1 block’s bottom face and oriented downward will always extend 0.25 units into that block due to the 0.5 mounting offset and 0.75 inlet-side length. With a flush vertical stack over the base, the cannon’s body z-span [0.5, 2.25] also overlaps the underlying riser (Block A) and base.
- Therefore, the plan’s Step 3 (bottom-face mount of the cannon under Block B, with the stack centered over the base) is incompatible with the no-overlap requirement.

Corrective recommendations (one viable path)
- Mount the Water Cannon on a vertical side face of Block B (east or west), still oriented to point down (−z). This avoids the intrinsic host-block intrusion created by bottom-face mounting.
- Then reposition the Torch so its heating sphere overlaps the cannon inlet:
  • For a side-face-mounted cannon, the cannon center z is 2.5, inlet z = 3.25. The current torch placement on Block B’s top face yields heating sphere z = 2.5, which is too far (Δz = 0.75 > 0.3).
  • Add one more small wooden block above Block B (a “Block C”) and attach the torch to Block C’s top face pointing down. With Block C center at z = 3.5, top face at z = 4.0 → torch body center at 4.5 → heating sphere center at 3.5 → Δz to inlet = |3.5 − 3.25| = 0.25 (< 0.3), satisfying steam heating.
- Also, laterally offset the entire unit at least 1.0 units in x or y from the base center to ensure the cannon’s 1×1 cross section does not overlap the 1×1 base footprint, since the cannon occupies [0.5, 2.25] in z.
- Preserve Block A and Block B west/east faces for bracing as required.

If bottom-face mounting is absolute (cannot be changed):
- Then the design is untenable under the no-overlap rule. Bottom-face mounting of this cannon into a 1×1×1 solid will always intrude 0.25 into that solid, and with the present stack it necessarily overlaps the base and Block A. You would need a non-solid host (e.g., a cutout or a different mount geometry) not available in the current block set.

Decision
- FAIL: The current blueprint does not meet the structural requirements due to multiple overlaps caused by the Water Cannon placement. Please revise the mounting approach (preferably side-face mount for the cannon plus adjusted torch placement as outlined) and provide updated coordinates so I can re-run the structural and functional checks.

title: drafter message
summary: Revision to resolve overlaps and preserve bracing faces Position adjustment note: - Moved Water Cannon from Block B bottom face to Block B north face (y+), still pointing straight down, to eliminate host intrusion and o…
body:
Revision to resolve overlaps and preserve bracing faces

Position adjustment note:
- Moved Water Cannon from Block B bottom face to Block B north face (y+), still pointing straight down, to eliminate host intrusion and overlaps with Base and Block A while keeping Block A/B west/east faces free for bracing.
- Added Small Wooden Block C above Block B to raise the Torch so its heating sphere overlaps the Water Cannon inlet in side-mount configuration without entering the plume.
- Functional intent unchanged: single-direction upward thrust via downward-pointing steam Water Cannon; Torch heats inlet to trigger steam mode; water cannon axis remains exactly vertical.

Blueprint declaration (build each new block at the center of an unoccupied face of the previous block)

1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25); global reference with center at (0, 0, 0.5) - starting reference

2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 1

3 - Small Wooden Block B - second vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 2

4 - Small Wooden Block C - torch riser to align heating sphere with side-mounted cannon inlet - top face of Block 3

5 - Water Cannon - downward-pointing propulsion unit; mounted on a vertical side to avoid intrusion; center offset 0.5 outward from face - north face of Block 3 - fires straight down (negative z) producing upward recoil; configure to shared Fire control; intended to operate in steam mode via torch heating of inlet

6 - Torch - downward-facing heater; center offset 0.5 above the attached top face; flame/heating sphere 1.0 below body center (radius 0.3) - top face of Block 4 - flame points straight down (negative z); no control (remains lit); heats Water Cannon inlet to trigger steam mode while staying clear of the downward plume

Assembly and coordinate notes (using global frame: +x east, +y north, +z up)

- Base (Block 1): center (0, 0, 0.5); z extents [0.0, 1.0].
- Block A (Block 2): attached on top of Base; center (0, 0, 1.5); z extents [1.0, 2.0]. West/East faces unused.
- Block B (Block 3): attached on top of A; center (0, 0, 2.5); z extents [2.0, 3.0]. West/East faces unused.
- Block C (Block 4): attached on top of B; center (0, 0, 3.5); z extents [3.0, 4.0].

Water Cannon (Block 5):
- Attachment: Block 3 north face (vertical; face center at (0, +0.5, 2.5)).
- Center: (0, +1.0, 2.5) due to 0.5 offset outward along +y.
- Axis and endpoints along z: inlet z = 2.5 + 0.75 = 3.25; outlet z = 2.5 − 1.0 = 1.5.
- Occupied spans: x ∈ [−0.5, +0.5], y ∈ [+0.5, +1.5], z ∈ [1.5, 3.25].
- Overlap checks:
  • vs Base: separated in z (cannon z ≥ 1.5, base z ≤ 1.0) → no overlap.
  • vs Block A: separated along y (center Δy = 1.0 equals half-width sum 1.0 → face contact only); no overlap.
  • vs Block B (host): separated along y (center Δy = 1.0 equals half-width sum 1.0 → face contact only); no overlap.
- Lowest outlet z = 1.5 ≥ 0.5 → meets “no ground contact” requirement.
- Axis perfectly vertical, pointing −z.

Torch (Block 6):
- Attachment: Block 4 top face; body center (0, 0, 4.5) via 0.5 offset above top face.
- Heating sphere center (flame): (0, 0, 3.5); radius 0.3.
- Proximity to Water Cannon inlet: inlet at z = 3.25; |Δz| = 0.25 < 0.3 → inlet lies inside heating sphere, ensuring steam mode.
- Torch body and shaft occupy free space above Block C; clear of cannon body; sphere sits above plume region.

Controls and behavior
- Water Cannon: configure to shared Fire input; when active in steam mode, delivers ~13.76 units of mass-equivalent upward thrust.
- Torch: no control; remains lit until extinguished; positioned to avoid direct exposure to downward steam plume while heating the cannon inlet.

Reserved faces for later bracing
- Block A and Block B west/east faces remain unoccupied, as required for chassis bracing.
- Cannon uses Block B north face (y+), not west/east.

Micro-adjustment readiness
- If later chassis integration reveals conflicts, we can:
  • Swap the cannon to the south face (y−) with equivalent geometry (center (0, −1.0, 2.5); inlet z 3.25; outlet z 1.5) without changing function.
  • Nudge torch mounting ±0.1 in x/y (by rotating its horizontal support) while keeping the heating sphere centered at (0, 0, 3.5), if needed to avoid nearby parts.

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Review assumptions and coordinate frame - Global frame: +x east, +y north, +z up. - Base block (1×1×1) center is at (0, 0, 0.5). All 1×1×1 blocks have half-extents (0.5, 0.5, 0.5). - Water Cannon oriented vertically (ax…
body:
Review assumptions and coordinate frame
- Global frame: +x east, +y north, +z up.
- Base block (1×1×1) center is at (0, 0, 0.5). All 1×1×1 blocks have half-extents (0.5, 0.5, 0.5).
- Water Cannon oriented vertically (axis along z), with overall axis length 1.75; we use half-extent along z = 0.875 and half-extents along x,y = 0.5.
- Torch mounting offsets per spec: body center sits 0.5 along the face normal from the face; heating sphere center is at body center + 1.0 along the torch’s “flame” direction. Sphere radius = 0.3.

Step-by-step structural integrity (positions, neighbor distances, overlap checks)

1) Block 1: Base (given)
- Center: (0, 0, 0.5); z-extents: [0.0, 1.0].
- Neighbors: none at this step.

2) Block 2: Small Wooden Block A
- Attachment: Base top (+z) face.
- Center: (0, 0, 1.5); z-extents: [1.0, 2.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs Base: Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0).
    - x: 0 < 1.0, y: 0 < 1.0, z: 1.0 = 1.0 → face contact only along z; no overlap. OK.
- Side-face reservation (A west/east): unoccupied. OK.

3) Block 3: Small Wooden Block B
- Attachment: A top (+z) face.
- Center: (0, 0, 2.5); z-extents: [2.0, 3.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs A: Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z; no overlap.
  • vs Base: Δ = (0, 0, 2.0) → separated; no overlap.
- Side-face reservation (B west/east): unoccupied. OK.

4) Block 4: Small Wooden Block C
- Attachment: B top (+z) face.
- Center: (0, 0, 3.5); z-extents: [3.0, 4.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs B: Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z; no overlap.
  • vs A/Base: clearly separated in z; no overlap.

5) Block 5: Water Cannon (mounted on B north face, axis vertical, firing −z)
- Attachment face: B north (+y) face; face center = (0, 0.5, 2.5).
- Cannon center (0.5 outward along +y): (0, 1.0, 2.5).
- Axis endpoints along z:
  • Inlet: z = 2.5 + 0.75 = 3.25.
  • Outlet: z = 2.5 − 1.0 = 1.5.
- AABB spans: x ∈ [−0.5, +0.5], y ∈ [+0.5, +1.5], z ∈ [1.5, 3.25].
- Neighbor checks (using center deltas and half-sums with cannon half-extents 0.5, 0.5, 0.875):
  • vs Base (0, 0, 0.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 2.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375).
    - x: 0 < 1.0; y: 1.0 = 1.0 (contact only); z: 2.0 > 1.375 → at least one axis separated → no overlap. Also by spans: base z ≤ 1.0, cannon z ≥ 1.5. OK.
  • vs A (0, 0, 1.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375).
    - x: 0 < 1.0; y: 1.0 = 1.0 (contact only); z: 1.0 < 1.375 (overlap in z).
    - Since y is exact face contact (no overlap), overall volumes do not intersect. OK.
  • vs B (0, 0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 0.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375).
    - x: 0 < 1.0; y: 1.0 = 1.0 (face contact to host); z: 0.0 < 1.375 (overlap in z).
    - As expected: face contact to its host with overlap in other axes is acceptable; no volumetric interpenetration because along y it’s exactly at the face. OK.
  • vs C (0, 0, 3.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375).
    - y: 1.0 = 1.0 (contact only); z overlaps; overall non-intersecting due to face contact in y. OK.
- Ground clearance: outlet z = 1.5 ≥ 0.5 → meets “no ground contact” requirement.

6) Block 6: Torch (as declared by drafter)
- Attachment: C top (+z) face, pointing downward (−z).
- Torch body center: (0, 0, 4.5).
- Heating sphere center: (0, 0, 3.5); radius 0.3.
- Neighbor checks (collisions):
  • vs C: torch body is offset 0.5 above top face per spec → no intrusion into C. OK.
  • vs Water Cannon: cannon max z = 3.25; torch body center z = 4.5. Clear separation; no overlap. The heating sphere is not a solid collider, so only functional proximity matters.

Structural integrity summary
- No overlaps detected among solid parts in the revised geometry. Host/face contacts are correct.
- Reserved bracing faces: A and B west/east faces remain unused. OK.

Functional validation

1) Motion and orientation
- Water Cannon axis is vertical, firing straight down (−z). Recoil thrust direction is straight up (+z). Satisfies the “keep the water cannon axis exactly vertical” requirement.
- Wheel and other moving elements: none. No rotation constraints to check.

2) Heating and steam-mode activation
- Water Cannon inlet location: (0, 1.0, 3.25).
- Torch heating sphere center: (0, 0, 3.5); radius = 0.3.
- 3D distance from heating sphere center to inlet:
  • Δ = (0, −1.0, +0.25); |Δ| = sqrt(0^2 + 1.0^2 + 0.25^2) ≈ 1.031.
  • 1.031 > 0.3 → The heating sphere does NOT reach the inlet. Steam mode will not trigger with the current torch placement.
- The prior z-only comparison is insufficient; the y-offset introduced by side-mounting the cannon must be considered. This is a functional failure.

3) Clearance from plume
- Cannon outlet at z = 1.5; torch heating sphere center at z = 3.5. The torch is well above the nozzle and out of the direct downward plume path. No extinguishing risk from the vertical plume in nominal operation.

Physical feasibility
- With the current torch placement, the cannon will operate in water mode only (recoil equivalent 1.6 units), not the intended steam mode (8.6×). Aside from this, structural massing and clearances are feasible.

Additional note on assembly sequencing statement
- Your “build each new block at the center of an unoccupied face of the previous block” is internally contradicted by Step 5 (Water Cannon attached to Block 3 while the previous block is Block 4). This does not affect build feasibility in this system (you may attach a new part to any existing block), but it should be clarified if you’re enforcing a strict step-by-step attachment rule.

Decision
- FAIL: Functional requirement not met. The torch’s heating sphere does not intersect the water cannon inlet; steam mode cannot be confirmed.

Minimal corrective change to meet steam-mode requirement while preserving clearances and reserved faces
- Add one more Small Wooden Block D atop Block C (center at (0, 0, 4.5)).
- Move the Torch to the north (+y) face of Block D, oriented to point downward (−z):
  • Torch body center becomes (0, 1.0, 4.5) (0.5 outward from D’s north face).
  • Heating sphere center becomes (0, 1.0, 3.5).
  • Distance to inlet (0, 1.0, 3.25) is |Δ| = 0.25 < 0.3 → inlet is inside the heating sphere; steam mode achieved.
- Collision checks with this change:
  • Block D vs cannon: Block D spans z ∈ [4.0, 5.0] and y ∈ [−0.5, 0.5]; cannon max z = 3.25 and y ∈ [0.5, 1.5]. They are separated in z (no overlap).
  • Torch vs cannon: y matches (1.0), but torch body is at z = 4.5 while cannon max z = 3.25 → separated in z.
  • Reserved faces (A and B west/east): remain free.

If you update the blueprint accordingly (add Block D as above and relocate the torch), I can re-run the full structural and functional checks.

title: drafter message
summary: Position adjustment note: - Adopted reviewer’s corrective change to meet steam-mode heating: added Small Wooden Block D above the stack and relocated the Torch to Block D’s north face, pointing downward. This places the…
body:
Position adjustment note:
- Adopted reviewer’s corrective change to meet steam-mode heating: added Small Wooden Block D above the stack and relocated the Torch to Block D’s north face, pointing downward. This places the heating sphere at (0, 1.0, 3.5), overlapping the Water Cannon inlet at (0, 1.0, 3.25) by 0.25 (< 0.3 radius).
- Retained the Water Cannon side-mount on Block B’s north face with axis vertical and pointing down to eliminate host intrusion and avoid overlaps with Base and Block A.
- Clarification: Functional parts may be attached to any existing block; “relative position” calls out the actual host block’s face for clarity even if it is not the immediately previous index.

Blueprint declaration

1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25), center at (0, 0, 0.5) - starting reference

2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 1

3 - Small Wooden Block B - second vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 2

4 - Water Cannon - downward-pointing propulsion unit mounted on a vertical side; center offset 0.5 outward from host face - north face of Block 3 - fires straight down (negative z); axis exactly vertical; recoil thrust straight up; configure to respond to shared Fire control; intended to operate in steam mode by torch heating of inlet

5 - Small Wooden Block C - torch standoff riser - top face of Block 3

6 - Small Wooden Block D - torch standoff riser (additional height to align heating sphere with side-mounted cannon inlet) - top face of Block 5

7 - Torch - downward-facing heater; body center sits 0.5 outward from host face; heating sphere center 1.0 below body center (radius 0.3) - north face of Block 6 - flame points straight down (negative z); no control (remains lit); heats Water Cannon inlet to trigger steam mode while staying clear of the downward plume

Assembly and coordinate notes (global frame: +x east, +y north, +z up)
- Block 1 (Base): center (0, 0, 0.5); z-extents [0.0, 1.0].
- Block 2 (A): center (0, 0, 1.5); z-extents [1.0, 2.0]. West/East faces unused.
- Block 3 (B): center (0, 0, 2.5); z-extents [2.0, 3.0]. West/East faces unused.
- Block 5 (C): center (0, 0, 3.5); z-extents [3.0, 4.0].
- Block 6 (D): center (0, 0, 4.5); z-extents [4.0, 5.0].

Water Cannon (Block 4):
- Host face: Block 3 north (+y); face center (0, 0.5, 2.5); cannon center (0, 1.0, 2.5) via 0.5 offset outward.
- Axis endpoints: inlet z = 2.5 + 0.75 = 3.25; outlet z = 2.5 − 1.0 = 1.5.
- Occupied spans: x ∈ [−0.5, +0.5], y ∈ [+0.5, +1.5], z ∈ [1.5, 3.25].
- Clearances: no overlap with Base (base z ≤ 1.0), Block A (contact only along +y), Block B (host face contact only). Ground clearance OK: outlet z = 1.5 ≥ 0.5.

Torch (Block 7):
- Host face: Block 6 north (+y); torch body center (0, 1.0, 4.5) via 0.5 outward from face.
- Heating sphere center: (0, 1.0, 3.5); radius 0.3.
- Heating coverage: Water Cannon inlet at (0, 1.0, 3.25); distance = 0.25 < 0.3 → inlet lies inside heating sphere; steam mode assured.
- Separation: torch body center z = 4.5; cannon max z = 3.25 → no overlap; torch remains above and out of the downward plume.

Controls and behavior
- Water Cannon (Block 4): assign shared Fire input; in steam mode delivers ~13.76 units of mass-equivalent upward thrust (8.6× water mode).
- Torch (Block 7): no control; remains lit until extinguished; positioned to heat the inlet and minimize extinguishing by the plume.

Reserved faces for later bracing
- Block A and Block B west/east faces remain unoccupied for brace connections to the chassis, per design requirements.

Micro-adjustment readiness
- If later integration requires, the cannon can be mirrored to Block 3 south (y−) face with identical z endpoints (center at (0, −1.0, 2.5); inlet z 3.25; outlet z 1.5); the torch can be mirrored to Block 6 south face accordingly (heating sphere center (0, −1.0, 3.5)). Functional intent remains unchanged.

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Review assumptions and frame - Global axes: +x east, +y north, +z up. - Base block (1×1×1) center at (0, 0, 0.5). All small wooden blocks are 1×1×1 with half-extents (0.5, 0.5, 0.5). - Water Cannon is mounted so its axi…
body:
Review assumptions and frame
- Global axes: +x east, +y north, +z up.
- Base block (1×1×1) center at (0, 0, 0.5). All small wooden blocks are 1×1×1 with half-extents (0.5, 0.5, 0.5).
- Water Cannon is mounted so its axis is vertical (along z). Geom for collision checks: half-extents (x=0.5, y=0.5), and along z we use a center-based half-length of 0.875 for separation tests, but also track its asymmetric end points (center−1.0 to center+0.75).
- Torch mounting offsets per spec: body center sits 0.5 outward from the host face along the face normal; heating sphere center is at body center + 1.0 along its flame direction; sphere radius = 0.3. For collision estimates, we conservatively use torch half-extents (0.25, 0.25, 0.25) because the spec’s examples guarantee the body is fully outside the host at 0.5 offset and the torch is slender relative to 1×1 blocks.

Step-by-step structural integrity (positions, neighbor distances, and overlap checks)

1) Block 1: Base (1×1×1)
- Center: (0, 0, 0.5); z-span [0.0, 1.0].
- Neighbors: none. Overlaps: none.

2) Block 2: Small Wooden Block A (1×1×1)
- Attachment: Base top (+z).
- Center: (0, 0, 1.5); z-span [1.0, 2.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs Base center (0, 0, 0.5): Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z only; no overlap.

3) Block 3: Small Wooden Block B (1×1×1)
- Attachment: A top (+z).
- Center: (0, 0, 2.5); z-span [2.0, 3.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs A (0, 0, 1.5): Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z only; no overlap.
  • vs Base (0, 0, 0.5): Δ = (0, 0, 2.0) ≥ half-sums → separated; no overlap.

4) Block 4: Water Cannon (mounted on B north face; axis vertical; points −z)
- Host face: B north (+y); face center (0, 0.5, 2.5).
- Cannon center: (0, 1.0, 2.5) (0.5 outward along +y).
- Endpoints along z: outlet z = 2.5 − 1.0 = 1.5; inlet z = 2.5 + 0.75 = 3.25. Occupied spans: x ∈ [−0.5, +0.5], y ∈ [+0.5, +1.5], z ∈ [1.5, 3.25].
- Neighbor checks (half-extents: cannon 0.5, 0.5, 0.875; cubes 0.5 each axis):
  • vs Base (0, 0, 0.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 2.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375). Separated along z (2.0 > 1.375); no overlap. Also z-spans disjoint (base z ≤ 1.0; cannon z ≥ 1.5).
  • vs A (0, 0, 1.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375). y is exact face contact (1.0 = 1.0), so despite x/z overlap, volumes do not interpenetrate. No overlap.
  • vs B (0, 0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 0.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375). Face contact to host along y; no overlap.
- Ground clearance: outlet z = 1.5 ≥ 0.5 → meets “no ground contact” requirement.

5) Block 5: Small Wooden Block C (1×1×1)
- Attachment: B top (+z).
- Center: (0, 0, 3.5); z-span [3.0, 4.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs B (0, 0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z; no overlap.
  • vs A (0, 0, 1.5): Δ = (0, 0, 2.0) → separated; no overlap.
  • vs Water Cannon (0, 1.0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375). y is exact face contact (1.0 = 1.0); no overlap.

6) Block 6: Small Wooden Block D (1×1×1)
- Attachment: C top (+z).
- Center: (0, 0, 4.5); z-span [4.0, 5.0].
- Neighbor checks:
  • vs C (0, 0, 3.5): Δ = (0, 0, 1.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.0) → face contact along z; no overlap.
  • vs Water Cannon (0, 1.0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 2.0); half-sums = (1.0, 1.0, 1.375). Separated along z (2.0 > 1.375); no overlap.
  • vs B/A/Base: larger z separations → no overlaps.

7) Block 7: Torch (attached to D north face; flame points −z)
- Host face: D north (+y). Torch body center: (0, 1.0, 4.5) (0.5 outward from face).
- Heating sphere center (flame): (0, 1.0, 3.5); radius 0.3.
- Neighbor checks (torch half-extents used: 0.25, 0.25, 0.25):
  • vs D (0, 0, 4.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 0.0); half-sums = (0.75, 0.75, 0.75). Separated along y (1.0 > 0.75); no overlap.
  • vs C (0, 0, 3.5): Δ = (0, 1.0, 1.0); half-sums = (0.75, 0.75, 0.75). Separated along both y and z; no overlap.
  • vs Water Cannon (0, 1.0, 2.5): Δ = (0, 0.0, 2.0); half-sums = (0.75, 0.75, 1.125). Separated along z (2.0 > 1.125); no overlap.

Structural integrity summary
- All solid parts are either face-contacting or separated on at least one axis; no volumetric interpenetration detected.
- Reserved faces: Block A and Block B west/east faces remain unoccupied for future bracing. OK.

Functional validation

1) Orientation and motion
- Water Cannon axis exactly vertical, firing straight down (−z). Recoil is straight up (+z) as required.
- Lowest outlet at z = 1.5 ≥ 0.5 satisfies ground-clearance at placement.

2) Heating and steam mode
- Water Cannon inlet: (0, 1.0, 3.25).
- Torch heating sphere center: (0, 1.0, 3.5); radius 0.3.
- Distance inlet to heating center: |Δ| = 0.25 < 0.3 → inlet lies inside heating sphere. Steam mode is reliably triggered.

3) Interference risks and mounting
- Torch sits well above the cannon body (torch body z = 4.5; cannon z ≤ 3.25) and is aligned along the same y. It is out of the downward plume path and unlikely to be extinguished during nominal vertical thrust.
- Water Cannon has no attachable faces used by other parts; mounting to B’s north face is valid.

Physical feasibility
- Mass estimate: 4 small wooden blocks × 0.3 = 1.2; Water Cannon 1.5; Torch 1.0; Base 0.25 → total ≈ 3.95 units. Steam-mode recoil ≈ 8.6 × 1.6 = 13.76 mass-equivalent thrust upward. One unit provides ample thrust margin for its own mass; paired units (east/west) will provide symmetric lift as intended.

Conclusion
- PASS: The revised blueprint meets structural integrity (no overlaps), functional requirements (vertical axis, ground clearance, heating for steam mode), and physical feasibility. The bracing faces remain available.

TERMINATE

Position adjustment note:
- Moved Water Cannon to Block B’s north face (y+) with axis vertical and firing downward to eliminate host intrusion and base overlap.
- Added Small Wooden Block D above Block C and relocated the Torch to Block D’s north face, pointing downward, so its heating sphere center (0, 1.0, 3.5) overlaps the Water Cannon inlet (0, 1.0, 3.25) by 0.25 (< 0.3), meeting steam-mode activation.

1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25), center at (0, 0, 0.5) - starting reference

2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 1

3 - Small Wooden Block B - second vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 2

4 - Water Cannon - downward-pointing vertical-axis propulsion unit; center offset 0.5 outward from host face - north face of Block 3 - fires straight down (negative z); recoil thrust straight up; configure to shared Fire control; operate in steam mode via inlet heating. Coordinates: center (0, 1.0, 2.5); inlet z = 3.25; outlet z = 1.5 (≥ 0.5 ground clearance).

5 - Small Wooden Block C - torch standoff riser - top face of Block 3

6 - Small Wooden Block D - additional torch standoff riser to align heating sphere with cannon inlet - top face of Block 5

7 - Torch - downward-facing heater; body center 0.5 outward from host face; heating sphere center 1.0 below body (radius 0.3) - north face of Block 6 - flame points straight down (negative z); no control (remains lit); heats Water Cannon inlet to trigger steam mode while staying clear of the downward plume. Coordinates: body center (0, 1.0, 4.5); heating sphere center (0, 1.0, 3.5), 0.25 above cannon inlet.

body:
The plan of the specific sub-structure 2: {'name': 'Symmetric Central Chassis', 'description': 'A minimal vertical frame to which two identical engine units are braced symmetrically to the east and west. The chassis aligns its side faces at the same two heights as the engine’s stacked wooden blocks for rigid dual-point bracing per engine. This maintains symmetry and reduces trajectory deviation.', 'components': '- Base: Default 1x1x1 stone block (mass 0.25)
        - Structural: Small Wooden Block C (1x1x1, mass 0.3), Small Wooden Block D (1x1x1, mass 0.3), stacked vertically
        - Connectors: 4 Braces (mass 0.5 each) for engine-to-chassis links (two per engine, upper and lower)', 'assembly': 'Build relative to the chassis base stone and then assemble engines:
        - Step 1: Place the chassis base stone so its bottom rests at z = 0 (world origin for the assembled rocket). Its center is at (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0.5).
        - Step 2: Attach Small Wooden Block C to the chassis base’s top (z+) face; C’s center at z = 1.5.
        - Step 3: Attach Small Wooden Block D to the top (z+) face of C; D’s center at z = 2.5.
        - Step 4: Position the two completed Engine Units:
          • East Engine: place its base stone so its center is at (x = +3.0, y = 0.0, z = 0.5). This sets its Block A center at z = 1.5 and Block B center at z = 2.5; the water cannon outlet at z = 0.5.
          • West Engine: place its base stone so its center is at (x = −3.0, y = 0.0, z = 0.5), mirroring the east engine.
        - Step 5: Connect Braces (each between free faces of wooden blocks):
          • East lower brace: from the West face center of East Engine Block A (x = +2.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5) to the East face center of Chassis Block C (x = +0.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5).
          • East upper brace: from the West face center of East Engine Block B (x = +2.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5) to the East face center of Chassis Block D (x = +0.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5).
          • West lower brace: from the East face center of West Engine Block A (x = −2.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5) to the West face center of Chassis Block C (x = −0.5, y = 0.0, z = 1.5).
          • West upper brace: from the East face center of West Engine Block B (x = −2.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5) to the West face center of Chassis Block D (x = −0.5, y = 0.0, z = 2.5).
        This creates two rigid, mirrored brace pairs, holding both engines symmetrically on either side of the central chassis.', 'motion_control': '- No active motion blocks in the chassis. It provides passive structural alignment. Ensure both engines’ water cannons are mapped to the same Fire control input to operate simultaneously.', 'function': 'Provides structural integrity and a symmetry reference for mounting the engines. The dual-height bracing per engine reduces flex, keeps thrust vectors parallel and centered, and minimizes trajectory deviation.', 'design_requirements': '- Keep Block C and Block D side faces free and aligned with the engine Blocks A and B to accept braces.
        - Braces must connect between unoccupied wooden block faces, not to water cannons or torches.
        - Position the two engines equidistant (±3.0 in x) from the chassis centerline for symmetry.
        - Ensure all components’ lowest points remain at z ≥ 0 to avoid ground intersection.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.'}

title: drafter message
summary: 1 - Default Starting Block - Chassis base block (default 1x1x1). Note: center at [0.0, 0.0, 0.5], bottom flush at z = 0.0 - N/A (starting block) 2 - Small Wooden Block - Chassis Block C (lower brace plane; keep east/wes…
body:
1 - Default Starting Block - Chassis base block (default 1x1x1). Note: center at [0.0, 0.0, 0.5], bottom flush at z = 0.0 - N/A (starting block)

2 - Small Wooden Block - Chassis Block C (lower brace plane; keep east/west faces free) - top face of Block 1 - static structural placement; center at [0.0, 0.0, 1.5]

3 - Small Wooden Block - Chassis Block D (upper brace plane; keep east/west faces free) - top face of Block 2 - static structural placement; center at [0.0, 0.0, 2.5]

4 - Brace - East lower brace linking East Engine Block A to Chassis Block C - east face of Block 2 (Block C) - structural function: solid hinge/brace between East Engine Block A west face center [2.5, 0.0, 1.5] and Chassis Block C east face center [0.5, 0.0, 1.5]; holds east engine’s lower mount rigidly, maintains parallel thrust alignment

5 - Brace - East upper brace linking East Engine Block B to Chassis Block D - east face of Block 3 (Block D) - structural function: solid hinge/brace between East Engine Block B west face center [2.5, 0.0, 2.5] and Chassis Block D east face center [0.5, 0.0, 2.5]; holds east engine’s upper mount rigidly, reduces flex

6 - Brace - West lower brace linking West Engine Block A to Chassis Block C - west face of Block 2 (Block C) - structural function: solid hinge/brace between West Engine Block A east face center [-2.5, 0.0, 1.5] and Chassis Block C west face center [-0.5, 0.0, 1.5]; holds west engine’s lower mount rigidly, mirrors east side

7 - Brace - West upper brace linking West Engine Block B to Chassis Block D - west face of Block 3 (Block D) - structural function: solid hinge/brace between West Engine Block B east face center [-2.5, 0.0, 2.5] and Chassis Block D west face center [-0.5, 0.0, 2.5]; holds west engine’s upper mount rigidly, mirrors east side

Assembly alignment notes:
- External engine unit placement reference (for brace endpoints): East Engine base at [3.0, 0.0, 0.5]; West Engine base at [-3.0, 0.0, 0.5]. Engine Blocks A and B centers align at z = 1.5 and z = 2.5 respectively; braces above connect strictly to the wooden blocks (not to water cannons or torches).
- All components’ lowest points are at z ≥ 0 (base bottom at z = 0; wooden blocks span upward).
- No active motion blocks in this chassis; map both engines’ water cannons to the same Fire control to operate simultaneously.

Position adjustment note:
- None required at this stage. If later build execution shows interference with engine accessories, we will micro‑adjust brace anchor points by ±0.1 on X while keeping endpoints on the same faces to maintain symmetry and rigidity.

title: draft_reviewer message
summary: Review of Sub-Structure 2: Symmetric Central Chassis Assumptions and geometry used - Default starting block: 1x1x1 cube, center at (0, 0, 0.5), faces at x = ±0.5, y = ±0.5, z = 0 and 1. Treated as the chassis base. - Sm…
body:
Review of Sub-Structure 2: Symmetric Central Chassis

Assumptions and geometry used
- Default starting block: 1x1x1 cube, center at (0, 0, 0.5), faces at x = ±0.5, y = ±0.5, z = 0 and 1. Treated as the chassis base.
- Small Wooden Block: 1x1x1, center-to-face offset = 0.5 along any axis.
- Brace: no physical volume/collider; must connect two existing attachable faces; endpoints must lie on the respective face planes.
- Engine units (referenced for brace endpoints): Each engine has a base at (±3.0, 0.0, 0.5); Engine Block A center at (±3.0, 0.0, 1.5), Engine Block B center at (±3.0, 0.0, 2.5). These are 1x1x1 wooden blocks with faces at x = ±3.0 ± 0.5 as appropriate. Braces attach to these A/B blocks, not to cannons/torches.

Step-by-step structural placement and clearance checks

Block 1 (reference): Default starting block, chassis base
- Center: (0.0, 0.0, 0.5). Bottom at z = 0. No action required.

Block 2: Small Wooden Block C (lower brace plane)
- Placement: on top (+z face) of Block 1.
- Center: (0.0, 0.0, 1.5).
- Neighbor checks (1x1x1 vs 1x1x1; min non-overlap center spacing along any axis is 1.0):
  - To Block 1 center: Δ = (0, 0, +1.0). z distance = 1.0 = sum of half-heights; face-to-face contact, no overlap.
  - To Block 3 (D) center: Δ = (0, 0, −1.0). z distance = 1.0; face-to-face contact, no overlap.
  - To East Engine Block A (3.0, 0.0, 1.5): Δ = (+3.0, 0, 0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To West Engine Block A (−3.0, 0.0, 1.5): Δ = (−3.0, 0, 0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To East Engine Block B (3.0, 0.0, 2.5): Δ = (+3.0, 0, +1.0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To West Engine Block B (−3.0, 0.0, 2.5): Δ = (−3.0, 0, +1.0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
- Side faces (east/west) are free for braces as specified.

Block 3: Small Wooden Block D (upper brace plane)
- Placement: on top (+z face) of Block 2.
- Center: (0.0, 0.0, 2.5).
- Neighbor checks:
  - To Block 2 (C): Δ = (0, 0, +1.0). z distance = 1.0; face-to-face contact, no overlap.
  - To Block 1 (base): Δ = (0, 0, +2.0). z distance = 2.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To East Engine Block B (3.0, 0.0, 2.5): Δ = (+3.0, 0, 0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To West Engine Block B (−3.0, 0.0, 2.5): Δ = (−3.0, 0, 0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To East Engine Block A (3.0, 0.0, 1.5): Δ = (+3.0, 0, −1.0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
  - To West Engine Block A (−3.0, 0.0, 1.5): Δ = (−3.0, 0, −1.0). x distance = 3.0 > 1.0; no overlap.
- Side faces (east/west) are free for braces as specified.

Connectors (Braces): All have no volume; collision-free by definition. Validate endpoints and on-face placement:

Brace 4: East lower brace (Engine A west face to Chassis C east face)
- Endpoints given: (2.5, 0.0, 1.5) ↔ (0.5, 0.0, 1.5).
- On-face verification:
  - East Engine Block A center (3.0, 0.0, 1.5), west face plane x = 3.0 − 0.5 = 2.5 → OK.
  - Chassis Block C center (0.0, 0.0, 1.5), east face plane x = 0.0 + 0.5 = 0.5 → OK.
- Span/length: 2.0 along x. Valid two-block connection.

Brace 5: East upper brace (Engine B west face to Chassis D east face)
- Endpoints: (2.5, 0.0, 2.5) ↔ (0.5, 0.0, 2.5).
- On-face verification:
  - East Engine Block B center (3.0, 0.0, 2.5), west face x = 2.5 → OK.
  - Chassis Block D center (0.0, 0.0, 2.5), east face x = 0.5 → OK.
- Span/length: 2.0 along x. Valid two-block connection.

Brace 6: West lower brace (Engine A east face to Chassis C west face)
- Endpoints: (−2.5, 0.0, 1.5) ↔ (−0.5, 0.0, 1.5).
- On-face verification:
  - West Engine Block A center (−3.0, 0.0, 1.5), east face x = −3.0 + 0.5 = −2.5 → OK.
  - Chassis Block C center (0.0, 0.0, 1.5), west face x = −0.5 → OK.
- Span/length: 2.0 along x. Valid two-block connection.

Brace 7: West upper brace (Engine B east face to Chassis D west face)
- Endpoints: (−2.5, 0.0, 2.5) ↔ (−0.5, 0.0, 2.5).
- On-face verification:
  - West Engine Block B center (−3.0, 0.0, 2.5), east face x = −2.5 → OK.
  - Chassis Block D center (0.0, 0.0, 2.5), west face x = −0.5 → OK.
- Span/length: 2.0 along x. Valid two-block connection.

Global ground-clearance check
- All cubes: lowest z faces at z = 0 (chassis base and engine bases). All other elements are above z = 0. Braces have no volume; their endpoints lie at z = 1.5 and 2.5. Requirement z ≥ 0 satisfied.

Functional validation
- No active motion blocks on chassis; it serves as a rigid alignment frame.
- Dual-height brace pairs per engine create symmetric, two-point connections at z = 1.5 and z = 2.5. This substantially reduces flex and helps maintain parallel thrust vectors.
- Braces connect between unoccupied wooden faces as specified. Note: This is contingent on the engine Blocks A and B having their west/east faces free. From the provided engine placement reference, this is consistent.
- Control mapping note (both engines’ water cannons to same Fire input) is external to this sub-structure; no conflict detected here.

Physical feasibility assessment
- Geometry is symmetric about the chassis centerline; engine placements at x = ±3.0 are equidistant. Braces are equal length (2.0) and mirrored, so net structural and thrust symmetry is preserved.
- Face-to-face stacking of chassis blocks is correct; no overlaps detected.
- The plan reduces trajectory deviation by aligning bracing planes with engine block heights, providing moment resistance against pitch/yaw induced by recoil.

Issues/clarifications
- Micro-adjustment note: Adjusting brace endpoints by ±0.1 on X while “keeping endpoints on the same faces” is not valid. The x-coordinate of a face anchor is fixed by the face plane (e.g., x = 2.5 for the west face of an east engine block, x = 0.5 for the east face of a chassis block). If interference occurs, adjust along the face plane (y and/or z), not along x. This does not affect the current design but should be corrected for future iterations.
- Brace connectivity dependency: This sub-structure assumes the engine Blocks A and B exist at the stated coordinates. If those are not present at assembly time, the braces would violate the rule that a brace must connect two existing attachable faces.

Conclusion
- Structural integrity: Pass. All placements are collision-free, with proper face-to-face contacts and brace endpoints lying exactly on valid face planes.
- Functional implementation: Pass. Braces connect the correct faces at two heights per engine, maintaining symmetry and alignment. No improper attachments to water cannons or torches.
- Physical feasibility: Pass. Symmetry, clearances, and brace geometry are appropriate to minimize trajectory deviation.

Approval
The blueprint, as specified, meets the design requirements and passes all checks, with the advisory to adjust brace anchors along y/z (not x) if micro-adjustments are needed later.

TERMINATE

title: builder thought
summary: Toolset overview and how we’ll use them for your build: - functions.start: Creates the initial “starting block” of the machine at a specified position [x, y, z] and rotation [yaw, pitch, roll]. Provide init_shift (offse…
body:
Toolset overview and how we’ll use them for your build:
- functions.start: Creates the initial “starting block” of the machine at a specified position [x, y, z] and rotation [yaw, pitch, roll]. Provide init_shift (offset from origin), init_rotation, and a descriptive note. The note field is very important because it acts like a durable identifier for the block’s role (e.g., “Chassis base block”).
- functions.attach_block_to: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block. You must specify base_block (ID or name), face (“Top”, “Bottom”, “East”, “West”, “North”, “South”), new_block type (Small Wooden Block, Water Cannon, Powered Wheel, Torch), and a note. The note is a powerful identifier to mark the block’s function (e.g., “Chassis Block C (lower brace plane; keep east/west faces clear)”).
- functions.connect_blocks: Connects two blocks via a connector (Brace or Winch). You must specify the two blocks, their faces, the connector type, and a note. The note can describe the structural intent (e.g., exact endpoint coordinates, “solid hinge/brace between endpoints ...”), which is extremely useful for tracing and maintenance. Be aware the connection fails if faces are too close; use shift_block to micro-adjust if necessary.
- functions.shift_block: Shifts a block by a small offset vector [x, y, z] in the range [-0.5, 0.5]. Useful for micro-adjustments to avoid collisions or align braces, exactly like your ±0.1 on X plan. Provide block_id and the shift vector.
- functions.twist_block: Rotates a block around its rooted face by a given angle (degrees). Provide block_id and angle. Handy for orienting pointer-like blocks or ensuring faces point in the desired direction.
- functions.flip_spin: Flips spin direction of a block that has a spin property. Provide block_id. Not applicable to static wooden blocks/braces, but relevant to rotors or wheels.
- functions.remove_block: Removes a block entirely. Provide block_id. Use if we place something incorrectly.
- functions.reset: Clears all blocks to the initial empty machine state. Use only if we want to restart.
- functions.get_block_details: Returns full details (position, rotation, face info) for a specific block. Provide block_id. Use this to verify face labels, exact centers, and to plan precise connections.
- functions.get_machine_summary: Returns the current machine overview. It’s mandatory to run this before we finish the process to do a final check; I will remind you at the end.

Plan to execute your blueprint step by step, one block/action per message:
1) Create Block 1: Default Starting Block, noted as “Chassis base block (default 1x1x1; center at [0.0, 0.0, 0.5], bottom at z = 0.0)”.
2) Attach Block 2: Small Wooden Block on the Top face of Block 1, note “Chassis Block C (lower brace plane; keep east/west faces clear)”.
3) Attach Block 3: Small Wooden Block on the Top face of Block 2, note “Chassis Block D (upper brace plane; keep east/west faces clear)”.
4) Connect Brace from Block 2 East face to East Engine Block A West face; include your specified endpoint note.
5) Connect Brace from Block 3 East face to East Engine Block B West face; include endpoint note.
6) Connect Brace from Block 2 West face to West Engine Block A East face; include endpoint note.
7) Connect Brace from Block 3 West face to West Engine Block B East face; include endpoint note.
If later we detect interference, we will use shift_block to micro-adjust ±0.1 on X while keeping endpoints on the same faces, preserving symmetry and rigidity.

Proceeding with Step 1 now.

Step 2: Attach Small Wooden Block (Chassis Block D) to the top of Chassis Block C (ID 2).
- Target: ID 2 (Small Wooden Block), Face E (top face; face center at [0.0, 0.0, 1.5], facing up)

- Expected placement result: New block center at [0.0, 0.0, 2.0]; keep east/west faces clear for braces

After placement, please report the new block’s ID, position, and its attachable faces.

If so, please provide their IDs and confirm their attachable faces so we can install the braces correctly.   - West: ID 14 at [-3.0, 0.0, 1.0], ID 15 at [-3.0, 0.0, 2.0]
- Confirm chassis blocks:
  - ID 2 at [0.0, 0.0, 1.0], ID 3 at [0.0, 0.0, 2.0]

title: guidance message
summary: Analysis: The built structure matches the blueprint intent with a consistent Z-offset adaptation to the environment. - Environment uses starting block center at z = 0.0 (top face at z = 0.5), while the blueprint referen…
body:
Analysis: The built structure matches the blueprint intent with a consistent Z-offset adaptation to the environment.

- Environment uses starting block center at z = 0.0 (top face at z = 0.5), while the blueprint references a starting block center at z = 0.5 (top face at z = 1.0). We adjusted all brace plane Z coordinates by −0.5 accordingly. As a result:
  - Lower brace plane uses z = 1.0 (instead of 1.5 in the blueprint).
  - Upper brace plane uses z = 2.0 (instead of 2.5 in the blueprint).

Confirmed placements:
- Chassis blocks:
  - ID 2 at [0.0, 0.0, 1.0]
  - ID 3 at [0.0, 0.0, 2.0]
- East engine blocks:
  - ID 7 at [3.0, 0.0, 1.0]
  - ID 8 at [3.0, 0.0, 2.0]
- West engine blocks:
  - ID 14 at [-3.0, 0.0, 1.0]
  - ID 15 at [-3.0, 0.0, 2.0]

Braces and endpoints (all confirmed):
- East lower brace (ID 9): connects ID 2 east face [0.5, 0.0, 1.0] to ID 7 west face [2.5, 0.0, 1.0]
- East upper brace (ID 10): connects ID 3 east face [0.5, 0.0, 2.0] to ID 8 west face [2.5, 0.0, 2.0]
- West lower brace (ID 16): connects ID 2 west face [-0.5, 0.0, 1.0] to ID 14 east face [-2.5, 0.0, 1.0]
- West upper brace (ID 17): connects ID 3 west face [-0.5, 0.0, 2.0] to ID 15 east face [-2.5, 0.0, 2.0]

Symmetry and clearances:
- East/west faces of chassis blocks are only used by the intended braces; no other attachments interfere.
- Engine blocks are positioned exactly at x = ±3.0, with braces spanning 2.0 units horizontally at both lower and upper planes, maintaining symmetry.
- No interference/collision reported; no micro-adjustments were necessary.

Conclusion: The latest structure is consistent with the blueprint’s functional requirements (with the documented Z-offset adaptation). TERMINATE

title: user message
summary: <blueprint> Position adjustment note: - Moved Water Cannon to Block B’s north face (y+) with axis vertical and firing downward to eliminate host intrusion and base overlap. - Added Small Wooden Block D above Block C and…
body:
<blueprint>
Position adjustment note:
- Moved Water Cannon to Block B’s north face (y+) with axis vertical and firing downward to eliminate host intrusion and base overlap.
- Added Small Wooden Block D above Block C and relocated the Torch to Block D’s north face, pointing downward, so its heating sphere center (0, 1.0, 3.5) overlaps the Water Cannon inlet (0, 1.0, 3.25) by 0.25 (< 0.3), meeting steam-mode activation.

1 - Base Starting Block - default 1x1x1 cubic base (mass 0.25), center at (0, 0, 0.5) - starting reference

2 - Small Wooden Block A - first vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 1

3 - Small Wooden Block B - second vertical riser; reserve west/east faces for bracing - top face of Block 2

4 - Water Cannon - downward-pointing vertical-axis propulsion unit; center offset 0.5 outward from host face - north face of Block 3 - fires straight down (negative z); recoil thrust straight up; configure to shared Fire control; operate in steam mode via inlet heating. Coordinates: center (0, 1.0, 2.5); inlet z = 3.25; outlet z = 1.5 (≥ 0.5 ground clearance).

5 - Small Wooden Block C - torch standoff riser - top face of Block 3

6 - Small Wooden Block D - additional torch standoff riser to align heating sphere with cannon inlet - top face of Block 5

7 - Torch - downward-facing heater; body center 0.5 outward from host face; heating sphere center 1.0 below body (radius 0.3) - north face of Block 6 - flame points straight down (negative z); no control (remains lit); heats Water Cannon inlet to trigger steam mode while staying clear of the downward plume. Coordinates: body center (0, 1.0, 4.5); heating sphere center (0, 1.0, 3.5), 0.25 above cannon inlet.
<end blueprint>

title: builder thought
summary: Tool overview and how we will use them step by step: - functions.start - What it does: Creates the first base block and places it in the world with a defined initial shift [x, y, z] and rotation [yaw, pitch, roll]. - Wh…
body:
Tool overview and how we will use them step by step:

- functions.start
  - What it does: Creates the first base block and places it in the world with a defined initial shift [x, y, z] and rotation [yaw, pitch, roll].
  - What I need from you: Desired starting position and rotation if not default. A descriptive note is highly recommended; the note is a powerful identifier to mark the role of the block (e.g., “Base Starting Block”).
  - When we’ll use it: Step 1 to place the base block at center (0, 0, 0.5).

- functions.attach_block_to
  - What it does: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block. Faces are labeled (e.g., North, South, East, West, Top, Bottom). If you’re unsure of available faces or orientation, we can query get_block_details first.
  - What I need from you: The base block’s ID, the face to attach to, the type of new block, and a clear note. The note field is very important to record the block’s function, orientation, and design intent (e.g., “Small Wooden Block B – vertical riser” or “Water Cannon – steam-mode inlet alignment”).
  - When we’ll use it: Steps 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 to attach risers, the water cannon, and the torch to the specified faces.

- functions.twist_block
  - What it does: Rotates a block around its rooted surface by a specified angle (degrees). Useful for orienting “pointer” or directional blocks (like Torch flame or Water Cannon nozzle). For example, a pointer attached to a vertical face might point outward by default; twisting can reorient it to point downward (negative z) as required.
  - What I need from you: The block’s ID and the exact rotation angle. We may use get_block_details to verify current orientation before/after twisting. The note in attach_block_to should already describe intended orientation for traceability.
  - When we’ll use it: After attaching the Water Cannon and Torch to ensure they point straight down (negative z) if the default orientation needs correction.

- functions.shift_block
  - What it does: Applies a fine position offset [x, y, z] in the range [-0.5, 0.5] to the selected block. This is helpful to achieve exact coordinate alignment or to resolve overlaps after attachment.
  - What I need from you: The block’s ID and the precise offset vector. We’ll typically confirm via get_block_details first. Notes placed during attachment help remember why a shift was applied.
  - When we’ll use it: If necessary, to meet the blueprint’s specified centers (e.g., Water Cannon center at (0, 1.0, 2.5) and Torch sphere center at (0, 1.0, 3.5)).

- functions.connect_blocks
  - What it does: Connects two blocks using a connector (Winch or Brace) from specified faces. Fails if faces are too close.
  - What I need from you: IDs of both blocks, faces to connect, connector type, and a note explaining the purpose (e.g., “Brace on riser west/east faces reserved for bracing”).
  - When we’ll use it: Only if your blueprint later requires braces or winches. Your current plan reserves west/east faces for bracing but doesn’t specify a connection yet.

- functions.get_block_details
  - What it does: Returns complete details of a specific block (position, rotation, face info). Essential for precise orientation and alignment checks.
  - What I need from you: The block’s ID.
  - When we’ll use it: As needed after critical attachments (e.g., Water Cannon and Torch) to verify vertical axis and downward pointing, and exact centers/inlet/outlet positions.

- functions.get_machine_summary
  - What it does: Returns the latest overview of the machine. Does not include face captions, but gives a good structural snapshot.
  - Important: It is mandatory to use this tool for a final check before we end the build process. I will remind you at the end to run this check.
  - When we’ll use it: Final verification prior to termination; optionally we can also use it in-between to track IDs.

- functions.remove_block
  - What it does: Removes a block if a correction is needed.
  - What I need from you: The block’s ID and context for removal (our notes help identify the block quickly).
  - When we’ll use it: Only if we need to undo and rebuild a piece.

- functions.flip_spin
  - What it does: Flips spin direction on spinning blocks. Not applicable unless a block has spinning behavior.
  - What I need from you: The block’s ID.
  - When we’ll use it: Not planned in your current blueprint.

Plan to execute your blueprint one block at a time:
1) Start base block at default so its center is at (0, 0, 0.5).
2) Attach Small Wooden Block A to the Top face of the base.
3) Attach Small Wooden Block B to the Top face of Block A.
4) Attach Water Cannon to the North face of Block B, ensure vertical axis and firing downward; verify/reorient by twist if needed; check offsets and alignment to meet inlet/outlet and center coordinates.
5) Attach Small Wooden Block C to the Top face of Block B.
6) Attach Small Wooden Block D to the Top face of Block C.
7) Attach Torch to the North face of Block D, ensure it points straight down; verify the heating sphere center overlaps the Water Cannon inlet by 0.25 (< 0.3) for steam-mode activation.

Proceeding with Step 1: create the Base Starting Block with default placement and a descriptive note.
